county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Teller County formed 1899 from El Paso and Fremont counties. County seat is Cripple Creek. Named for Senator Henry Teller. Last great Colorado gold rush (Cripple Creek district, 1891). Most productive gold district in Colorado. Labor history (WFM strikes).
county_metadata.research_tips: Teller County Clerk and Recorder in Cripple Creek holds records from 1899 (created from El Paso and Fremont counties). The Cripple Creek mining district was the last great Colorado gold rush (1891-1900s) and produced more gold than any other district in Colorado. Cripple Creek, Victor, Goldfield, and Independence were mining towns with diverse populations. The Cripple Creek District Museum and the Victor-Lowell Thomas Museum have mining-era collections. The Western Federation of Miners was active here -- labor strike of 1894 and 1903-1904. Immigrant miners included Cornish, Irish, Italian, Finnish, and Scandinavian workers. Check El Paso and Fremont counties for pre-1899 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims. The Cripple Creek Times newspaper documents the mining era.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Teller County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Teller County Deed Records -- County Clerk and Recorder
source_profiles.repository: Teller County Clerk and Recorder, Cripple Creek
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books, mortgages, and liens for Teller County from 1899; County Clerk and Recorder maintains original records and indexes

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Washington County formed 1887 from Weld and Arapahoe counties. County seat is Akron. Named for President Washington. Eastern plains farming and ranching. Burlington Railroad. Homesteading era. Dust Bowl displacement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County Clerk and Recorder in Akron holds records from 1887 (created from Weld and Arapahoe counties). An eastern plains county with farming and ranching heritage. Akron was a Burlington Railroad town. The county experienced significant homesteading in the 1890s-1910s followed by Dust Bowl displacement in the 1930s. Washington County Museum in Akron has local collections. Check Weld and Arapahoe counties for pre-1887 records. BLM GLO homestead records are essential for tracking original settlers. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Church records (Methodist, Lutheran, Catholic) in Akron and Otis document rural families. The Akron News-Reporter newspaper is a source for vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Washington County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Weld County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Greeley. Named for Territorial Secretary Lewis Weld. Union Colony (1870) utopian agricultural settlement. Sugar beet farming. German-Russian, Hispanic, Japanese immigrants. Parent of many NE Colorado counties. Colorado State University.
county_metadata.research_tips: Weld County Clerk and Recorder in Greeley holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Greeley was founded in 1870 as the Union Colony, a utopian agricultural colony organized by Nathan Meeker and Horace Greeley (New York Tribune editor). The City of Greeley Museums (Meeker Home Museum) and Weld County Genealogical Society are important research resources. Weld County is one of the largest agricultural counties in Colorado -- sugar beet farming brought German-Russian, Hispanic, and Japanese immigrant workers. Great Western Sugar Company records document these communities. The University of Northern Colorado (founded 1889) in Greeley has archival collections. The Greeley Tribune newspaper (from 1870) is a rich source. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO homestead records are extensive. Weld County was the parent of many northeastern Colorado counties (Logan, Morgan, Washington, Phillips, Sedgwick).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Weld County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Yuma County formed 1889 from Washington and Arapahoe counties. County seat is Wray. Named from a Yuma word. Eastern plains farming and ranching. German-Russian settlers. Beecher Island Battlefield (1868). Dust Bowl displacement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yuma County Clerk and Recorder in Wray holds records from 1889 (created from Washington and Arapahoe counties). An eastern plains county with farming and ranching heritage. Wray and Yuma are the main towns. The county experienced homesteading in the 1890s-1910s followed by Dust Bowl displacement in the 1930s. German-Russian settlers established several farming communities. The Wray Museum has local history collections. The Beecher Island Battlefield (1868 battle between US Army and Cheyenne/Arapaho warriors) is in Yuma County. Check Washington and Arapahoe counties for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Church records (Lutheran, Catholic, Methodist) document rural families. The Wray Gazette newspaper.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Yuma County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov
